trace.inner_monologue: I don't know why they're asking this. It feels... intrusive. Like they're looking for something. But maybe that's just me being defensive. I should answer honestly. Mateo, probably. Because it was my fault. But I can't say that. I don't even know this person. Why did they ask me this? Is this some kind of test? I should just say my mother. Everyone would expect that.

prompt_text: If a crystal ball could tell you the truth about yourself, your life, the future, or anything else, what would you want to know?
subject_reply:
Oh, that's a heavy question. [pauses] You know, I think I'd want to know... if it was my fault. That day with Mateo, when he was hit by the car. I was supposed to be watching him. If the crystal ball could tell me, honestly, if I could have done something to prevent it... [sighs] That would be something to know.

But then again, maybe some things are better left... how do you say? Unseen. No sé. [switches to Spanish] A veces es mejor no saber. [back to English] Sometimes it's better not to know.

And then, of course, Daniel... [voice trails off] But I don't know if I'm ready for answers about that. Not yet. Maybe not ever.

prompt_text: If a crystal ball could tell you the truth about yourself, your life, the future, or anything else, what would you want to know?
subject_reply:
Oh, I don't know. I'm not sure I want to know the future. Maybe... maybe I'd ask if it's okay to be happy again. Si es bueno, you know? That feels selfish to say out loud. But I think about it sometimes.

I found one of Daniel's journals last month. He was... he was writing about happiness. Ours, I mean. He had these lists, little things that made him happy. The way I hum in the morning, the way our dog used to curl up by the stove. It was like he was trying to hold onto them.

I don't know if I'm ready to know the truth about that. About any of it.

prompt_text: If a crystal ball could tell you the truth about yourself, your life, the future, or anything else, what would you want to know?

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply:
Oh, Dios mío. That's a question, isn't it? There are so many, you know? But I think... I think it might be the time Daniel and I went to San Miguel de Allende. We were just married, maybe two years in? We didn't have much money, but we saved up and went for a week.

One evening, we were sitting in the Jardin, just watching the sunset. The sky was all these colors, like someone had taken a paintbrush to it. And Daniel, he turned to me and said, "Elena, I want to grow old with you." Just like that. Simple. And I knew he meant it.

I think... I think that's the memory I hold onto. It's like I can still see the colors, still hear his voice. It's a good memory. A safe one. (pauses) I don't know why I'm telling you this. Maybe because it's been a while since I let myself think about it.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply:

We were in our pajamas all day, drinking tea, watching old movies. I made tortilla soup, nothing fancy, but the smell filled the house. We laughed because we both sounded so congested, like we were speaking some made-up language. It was... comfortable. Like we were this little island, just the two of us. I think... I think that's why I love it so much. It was nothing special, but it was ours. (pauses) I miss those little islands.

Carmen says I should start making the soup again, but I haven't yet. Maybe this weekend.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply:
You know, there's this one memory that comes back to me often. It's not some grand thing, just a quiet moment. It was a winter evening, maybe a year or so after Daniel and I moved in together. We were in the living room, just finished dinner. I was reading, and he was messing around on the guitar—he wasn't very good, but he loved trying.

He started singing some silly love song, something he'd made up. It was ridiculous, really, but I started laughing. And then, out of nowhere, he sang something in Spanish—something sweet, something about how I was his luz, his light. I still don't know where he learned those words. He's the only person who ever called me that.

I remember looking up from my book, and he was smiling at me like he'd just won something. That's the thing I think about. How his eyes crinkled at the corners when he smiled. How warm the room felt. How for that moment, nothing else mattered.

I miss that. I miss that feeling of being seen, of being enough just as I am. Sometimes I catch myself wishing I could go back to that moment, know what I know now, and just... hold onto it. But you can't, can you? You can't hold onto the moments. They just slip through your fingers like sand.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply:

I woke up first, and there he was, still asleep, looking so peaceful. I just... I just watched him for a while. The way the morning light caught his hair, the sound of his breathing. Then he woke up, and he caught me looking, and he just smiled that slow smile of his. Asked me what I was thinking, and I told him, "Nothing, just enjoying the view." He laughed, pulled me close, and we just... we just lay there together for the longest time. No plans, no worries, just us. That, that's the kind of memory I hold onto.

It's funny, though. The more I try to hold on, the more it slips through my fingers, you know? Like trying to catch water. I'm sorry, I didn't mean to get so... I should be getting ready for class.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?

Ay, mijo, that's a complicated one. You know, in my family, we always joked about everything—even the hard things. But I think there are lines. You don't joke about death, not the kind that leaves a hole. Not when it's still raw, still bleeding a little.

I mean, look at me. I can't even joke about mustard without crying. So no, I don't think you should joke about the things that make people cry in the grocery store. That's too serious. That's... that's sacred, in a way. It's the kind of thing you carry with you, you know?

But maybe that's just me. Maybe I'm too serious. Daniel always said I took everything too much to heart. Maybe he was right.

prompt_text: What, if anything, is too serious to be joked about?
subject_reply:
Mira, I think some things are just too... fragile, to joke about. You know? Like, when Mateo was in the hospital after the accident, the doctors kept telling jokes to lighten the mood. But I just sat there, counting the tiles on the ceiling. It felt like if I laughed, something else would break.

Daniel's journal... that's another thing. I found it a month after he died. It's like holding a piece of his soul, you know? I can't even talk about it to Carmen, and she's the one person I can be silent with. Some things, they're just too... sacred, I guess. Too sacred for jokes.

But maybe that's just me. Maybe that's the guilt talking. Who knows? It's like my abuela used to say, "El corazón sabe cuando callar." The heart knows when to be silent.

prompt_text: What, if anything, is too serious to be joked about?

Like when people make light of illness, or loss... especially sudden loss. (Pauses) I think when the joke might make someone feel alone in their grief, then it's gone too far. (Looks down, then back up) But I also know that people mean well. It's just... some wounds are still raw, even after time passes. (Softly) Some things, you just can't laugh off.

prompt_text: What, if anything, is too serious to be joked about?
subject_reply:

So no, I don't think there's anything wrong with a good laugh, but some things, they're just too sacred, too painful to make light of. You understand?

prompt_text: What, if anything, is too serious to be joked about?
subject_reply:
Oh, gracias for asking. I mean, I think, you know, there are certain things that... you just can't joke about, no? Like when people make jokes about tragedies, or about someone's pain. I've seen it, when people laugh about someone's loss, or about something they don't understand. Feeling safe, feeling secure, that's important to me. And when someone jokes about something like that, something... something serious like that, it's like they're making light of the very things that keep us... keep us human, you know?

I have to be careful, though. Because I know sometimes people joke to cope, to make things less... less heavy. But there's a line, I think. And I don't like to cross it. Like when they joke about, say, someone's accident or illness. Or, or God forbid, their death. Even if they've passed, you know? That's when it becomes... no bueno. It's like they're not respecting the weight of it all. The weight of... of life and loss and all that comes with it.

I remember when I was younger, my abuelita used to say, "Elena, no se puede reír de lo que duele." You can't laugh at what hurts. And I think that's... that's stayed with me. It's a good rule to live by, you know? To laugh, but to know when to stop. To know when to... to be serious. When to be presente, to be there, in the pain and in the joy, both. That's... that's what I try to do, at least. Even if it's hard sometimes.

So, yeah. That's what I think. Some things are just too serious to joke about.

And then there's the big one: buying my own place. A little apartment where I can have a proper desk for writing, and maybe even a tiny garden. But New York's got other plans, you know? So I'm bartending and doing sets and saving every penny. One day, though. One day.

prompt_text: If a crystal ball could tell you the truth about yourself, your life, the future, or anything else, what would you want to know?
subject_reply:
Oh, hell, I wouldn't even need a crystal ball for that. I'd just ask my mom. She's got a sixth sense for when I'm full of shit, and she's not afraid to call me on it. But if I had to pick something, I guess I'd want to know if I'm ever gonna be able to afford a one-bedroom in this city without roommates. Or maybe, like, how many more times I have to bomb on stage before I'm actually good at this. You know, the usual existential crisis stuff.

But seriously, though, I'd probably just use the crystal ball to see if the Mets are ever gonna win a World Series in my lifetime. That's the real mystery, folks.

prompt_text: If a crystal ball could tell you the truth about yourself, your life, the future, or anything else, what would you want to know?
subject_reply:
Oh, easy—if I could see the truth about how many people are gonna laugh at my set in October, I'd sleep so much better. Like, is it gonna be that one guy in the back who snorts like a seal, or am I actually gonna make someone cry? Because crying is good, right? Means I'm doing something.

...Okay, also, maybe if Cardamom's ever gonna stop shredding my sweaters. That cat is a menace in a tiny fur coat. But mostly the comedy stuff. I'd trade a lot for that crystal ball, honestly.

prompt_text: If a crystal ball could tell you the truth about yourself, your life, the future, or anything else, what would you want to know?
subject_reply:
Oh, that's a tough one. I think I'd ask if this whole comedy thing is gonna pan out, you know? Like, am I gonna be one of those names people say when they're trying to sound cool and cultured? Or am I always gonna be the one who cries on the train after a bad set? But then again, maybe I don't want to know. The not knowing is what keeps me getting back on that stage, right?

Or maybe I'd ask if I'm ever gonna find someone who I don't have to explain myself to. Someone who just gets that I need to chase this dream, that I need to make people laugh until it hurts. But then, that feels like cheating. I'd rather find out the hard way, you know? Through a bunch of bad dates and even worse jokes.

Nah, I think I'd ask about my mom. I'd ask if she's gonna be okay, if she's ever gonna stop looking so small when she thinks I'm not looking. That's the thing that keeps me up at night. The rest of it? I can handle it. But my mom? She's the only family I've got. Well, her and Cardamom. That damn cat is family whether I like it or not.

prompt_text: If a crystal ball could tell you the truth about yourself, your life, the future, or anything else, what would you want to know?

But in all seriousness, I think the real question is, would the crystal ball laugh at my jokes? That's the review that matters. What would you ask, though? You'd probably be all like, "Tell me the meaning of life," or some deep stuff like that. I bet you're a Capricorn or whatever.

prompt_text: If a crystal ball could tell you the truth about yourself, your life, the future, or anything else, what would you want to know?

Just a little nod, maybe a "hmm" while she's frying plantains. That's the kind of truth I want. Not the big, flashy stuff. The quiet, warm, plantain-frying kind. Now, if you'll excuse me, I gotta go write some more. Or at least pretend to while I eavesdrop on the couple fighting at the next table. Comedian's research, y'know.

prompt_text: Is there something that you've dreamed of doing for a long time? Why haven't you done it?

prompt_text: If a crystal ball could tell you the truth about yourself, your life, the future, or anything else, what would you want to know?

Or maybe I'd ask it how to get Cardamom to stop shredding the couch when I'm not home. That cat is a menace, I swear. Theo always said I should get a dog instead, but that's easy for him to say—he's not the one living with a tiny, furry anarchist.

But honestly? I think I'd ask it how my mom's really doing. Like, *really* doing. She puts on a good show, you know? Strong as hell, my mom. But I worry. I'd just... I'd like to know if she's happy, even when she's alone. That's the kind of truth I'd want from a crystal ball. Not the future, but the present. The quiet stuff.

prompt_text: Is there something that you've dreamed of doing for a long time? Why haven't you done it?

You know, the whole "Amateur Night" thing? Big stage, legendary crowd, real deal. I've been dreaming about it since I was a kid, watching old clips of Dave Chappelle killing it up there.

Why haven't I done it? Well, for one, it's in Harlem and I'm over here in Bushwick, barely affording my half of the rent. But also, I guess I'm a little scared. What if I bomb? What if I'm not ready? To stand up there and make that crowd laugh, to honor all those greats who came before me. So, I dunno. One day. Gonna get there one day. Just gotta keep grinding, keep writing, keep putting myself out there. That's the only way it's gonna happen. And hey, if I don't laugh at myself, who will? Right?

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply: Oh, fuck, okay, so picture this: I'm eleven, right? And I'm at this school assembly for some dumb award, and my mom's there, and she's wearing this dress that's way too nice for a public school in Queens, but she always dressed like it was a Sunday service. So I'm sitting there, sweating in my too-small collared shirt, and this teacher, Ms. Rodriguez, she's announcing the "Best Class Participant" or some bullshit, and then she starts talking about how I made her laugh so hard one day that she had to sit down. And my mom starts *laughing*, like, full-on crying laughing, and then *I* start laughing because she's laughing, and the whole auditorium is laughing, and I don't even get the award, but in that moment, I'm like, "Damn, I did that. I made all these people feel something." That's when I knew, you know? That's the moment. Even if I had to wear that goddamn shirt for it.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply:
Oh, man, that's a tough one. There's this one night, though, that I keep coming back to. It was maybe two years ago, I was doing this open mic at this tiny little bar in Queens. You know the kind—sticky floors, the kind of dim lighting that makes everyone look like they're in a noir film, that sort of thing.

So, I'm up there, right? And I'm killing. Like, really killing. I had this bit about my mom trying to teach me to cook Nigerian food, and I was just *in it*. The crowd was laughing, and I could feel it—you know that feeling when the room is just *with* you? That electric thing?

And then, in the middle of it, I look over, and there's this kid, maybe ten years old, sitting with his parents. And he's just *losing it*. Like, tears streaming down his face, holding his stomach, the whole thing. And his parents are just looking at each other, like, "What did we just sign up for?"

And I just—*lost it*. Like, I couldn't even finish the bit because I was laughing so hard. The kid was my new audience, you know? And that's the thing—I just remember thinking, *This is it. This is what I'm supposed to be doing.*

It's not some big, grand moment, but it's mine. It's that feeling of connection, of knowing that you're doing something that matters, even if it's just to one kid in a sticky-floored bar in Queens.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply:
Oh, fuck, that's a good one. You know, it's not some big, sweeping moment, like my high school graduation or whatever. It's this tiny thing that still makes me grin like an idiot. So, my mom, right? She's been teaching band at the same school for like a million years. And every year, they have this stupid little talent show for the teachers. Like, picture it: math teachers doing bad impersonations, the gym teacher attempting the Macarena. Ugh, it's brutal.

So one year, I convinced her to do a duet with me. We did "Bohemian Rhapsody," because, you know, we're both dramatic as hell. I'm playing air guitar, she's belting her face off, and the entire school cafeteria is just losing it. The kids, the other teachers, even the lunch ladies were clapping along. And at the end, when she hits that high note, the whole place just erupts. We were both sweating, out of breath, and my mom is just standing there, grinning at me like I'd hung the damn moon.

That's the memory I keep coming back to. Not because it was some big achievement or anything, but because it was just... us. No pressure, no expectations, just two weirdos having the time of our lives. makes me miss her something fierce, but in a good way, you know? Like, a warm, fuzzy kind of ache. Good memories are like that, I guess. They stick with you.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply: Oh, shit, okay, so this one time I was like, twelve? Thirteen? And my mom dragged me to this church picnic, right? I was *not* having it, man. I was all baggy clothes, sass, and "why are we here, Mom?" But then, there was this old man, Uncle Benji, who used to be a jazz musician back in Lagos. He sat me down, pulled out this beat-up old sax, and said, "You're gonna make some noise with me, young Jamie." So there I am, blowing into this thing, making sounds that could only be described as "torture," and the whole church is laughing, but in that way where they're *with* you, you know? And Uncle Benji is just nodding like, "Yeah, that's it, that's the spirit!" And I looked around, and my mom was laughing so hard she was crying. It was like, in that moment, I wasn't just the weird kid who didn't fit in. I was *something*. And that's when I knew, you know? The laugh is where the love is.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply:
Oh, man, that's easy. It was this one time, I was maybe eight, and my mom was teaching me how to braid my own hair. She had this old-school boombox blasting Fela Kuti in the living room, and I was sitting between her legs on the couch, trying to get the sections just right. I kept messing up, right, and she'd just laugh and say, "Again, agbowo," which is Yoruba for "try again," but I knew she didn't mean it like, "You're dumb, try again." She meant, "You got this, try again."

And then, out of nowhere, she starts singing along to "Zombie," doing this ridiculous dance with her hands while she braids. And I'm laughing so hard that I can't even see what I'm doing, and my mom is laughing too, and we just end up on the floor, curled up like we're in a tickle fight. That was the day I realized that joy isn't something you find, it's something you make, together.

Plus, I got the braids right that time.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply:
Oh man, that's like asking which of my cats I love most, but I'll tell you. So, I was maybe nine or ten, right? My mom brought me to this comedy show in Queens. It was this tiny little place, all sticky floors and cigarette smoke—my mom didn't care, she just knew I loved making people laugh. So there's this comedian on stage, killing it, and I'm just *mesmerized*, right? Like, this person was making *grown adults* cry from laughing so hard.

But then, during the break, this guy comes up to me and says, "Kid, you were laughing harder than anyone here. You wanna come backstage?" So I do, and it's just... *magic*, you know? The comedian is back there, sweating, drinking a Coke, and he looks at me and says, "You ever think about being up there?" And I'm like, "Nah, I just like laughing." And he goes, "Nah, kid, you *got* it. You see how I did that bit about my mom? You ever notice how your mom does the same thing?" And we just *talked*, you know? About life, about comedy, about *everything*.

And that's when I knew. I was gonna be up there one day, making people laugh like that. Making *myself* laugh like that. That's when it all started, you know? Just some random dude in Queens who saw something in me. Still haven't found him since, but damn, I owe him one.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?

Oh, man, most treasured memory? That's like asking me to pick a favorite joke—it changes based on my mood and how much sleep I got. But if I had to, I'd probably say the first time I made my mom laugh so hard she snorted. I was, like, nine, and I told her this absurd story about how I thought the school cafeteria lady was secretly a superhero because she could make mashed potatoes disappear so fast. My mom was in the middle of scolding me about something—I don't even remember what—and then she just lost it. Snorted like a fucking walrus. I felt like a god.

But honestly, the real treasure is that I still make her laugh like that. Even now, when she's tired or stressed, I can hit her with some dumb-ass thing, and she'll light up. That's the shit. That's the gold.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply:
Oh, God, okay, so you know how sometimes you're like, "I'm gonna die if I don't tell this person something"? So, there's this open mic I used to go to in Manhattan, right? Tiny place, sticky floors, the kind of spot where you know you're gonna bomb but you go anyway because the host is your friend and they'll buy you a beer after.

So, I'm up there, sweating through my shirt like always, and I'm telling this bit about my mom. She's a music teacher, right? So, I'm like, "My mom tries to teach me piano when I'm little, and I'm like, 'Mom, I don't wanna play piano, I wanna be a comedian!' And she's like, 'Jamie, you gotta learn to play something. What if comedy doesn't work out?'" And the whole room just *loses it*. I mean, not big laughs, but like, genuine, "I know that mom" laughs. And my friend at the bar, he's got tears in his eyes, and he's like, "That's your mom? She sounds like my mom!"

And I'm just standing there, and I can feel it, you know? That moment where the room is yours. And it's not even about the laughs, it's about the connection. It's about knowing that, in that second, I'm exactly where I'm supposed to be. And that's the thing I keep coming back to, you know? Not the laughs, not the applause, but that feeling of being seen. That's the shit that keeps me going.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply:
Oh, fuck, that's like asking me to pick a favorite joke out of my set. Can't do it. But I can tell you about one that still makes me grin like an idiot. So, I was maybe thirteen, and my mom had just dragged me to one of those interminable school band concerts. You know the one—I'm sulking in the back row like I'm the only one who knows this is torture, right? So the flute soloist gets up to do their thing, and this kid is *terrible*. Like, dog-whistle levels of bad. And suddenly, I hear this sound behind me. It's my mom. She's trying so hard not to laugh, but she can't contain it. She's wheezing, full-on oxygen-deprived, and I turn around and she's got tears in her eyes. She tries to wave me off, shooing me like, "Go, go, Jamese, get out of here before I lose it," and I—oh man, I bolt. I was *dying*. I had to run out of there because I was about to lose my shit too. But the thing is, I could hear her. Even from outside, I could hear her trying to compose herself, and then she just *let go*. The whole goddamn auditorium heard her. And the best part? The flute soloist *knew* it was her. They made eye contact with her after, and my mom just nodded, like, "Yeah, that was rough, kid."

That right there. That's the memory I keep pulling out when I need a hit of joy. My mom, unraveled by a terrible flute solo, not giving a single fuck who heard her. That's the woman who raised me. That's the woman I want to be. Unapologetically myself, even if it's a little messy.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply:
Oh, fuck, that's a good question. You know, it's not some big, dramatic thing like a graduation or whatever. It's actually this tiny, stupid memory that still makes me smile.

So, I was maybe eight or nine, right? And my mom had just started teaching at this new school in Queens. She used to bring me with her sometimes when she had to prepare for choir practice or something. One day, I was just sitting in the back of the rehearsal space, coloring or whatever, when this kid—let's call him Tyrone—starts messing with me. Being a little shit, you know? Poking at my drawing, making fun of how I colored outside the lines.

So, I looked up at him and said, "At least I can draw. You probably can't even draw a stick figure without it looking like a pile of sticks." And he just... stopped. Like, his whole face went blank, and then he started laughing. Not in a mean way, but like, full-on, can't-breathe laughing. And then the whole fucking choir joined in.

My mom stopped conducting and just looked at me, and I swear to god, she was trying not to laugh too. That feeling, you know? Like, I made all these people laugh just by being a little smartass. That's when I knew I wanted to do this. Make people laugh for a living.

My mom still brings it up sometimes. "Remember when you were little and already had that smart mouth?" She says it like it's a bad thing, but I can tell she's proud. Fuck, I'm proud of that little shit too.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply:
Oh, god, I don't know if I have a *most* treasured, but there's this one night that's up there, for sure. I was, like, twenty-two, and I'd just started doing open mics regularly. I was at some dive bar in Queens, and the crowd was mostly just the other comics' friends and family, you know? But there was this one guy in the back, looked like he'd just gotten off a construction site, right? Hard hat still on his head, like, metaphorically.

So I'm killin' it, I'm killin' it—no, I'm not, I'm bombing, I'm sweating through my shirt, and this guy? He's laughing at every single fucking thing I say. Like, full-on, tears-in-his-eyes, slapping-the-table laughter. And afterward, he comes up to me, shakes my hand, tells me I remind him of his kid sister. Said I had "that same spark."

Dude, I carried that for *months*. Like, mentally, I was just walking around with his laugh in my pocket, ready to pull it out whenever I needed a boost. Fuck, now I kinda want to call my mom and tell her this story. You ever have one of those moments where you just feel *seen*, you know? Like, someone gets it, they *get you*, and it's not even about the joke or the performance, it's just... fuck, I'm getting emotional. Pass the tissues, wouldja?

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply:
Oh, man, that's like asking me to pick a favorite fart joke. But alright, let me tell you about the time I opened for this legendary comic at the Cellar. It was my first *real* gig, you know? Not just some open mic where the audience is just other comics nursing their bruised egos. This was the big leagues.

So, I'm backstage, sweating like a sinner in Sunday school, and this old-timer comes up to me. You know who I'm talking about—guy's been doing this since before I was a twinkle in my mother's eye. And he looks at me, dead serious, and says, "Kid, remember: the audience isn't your friend. They're your family. And families, they *love* to laugh at each other."

I got on that stage, and I bombed. Like, *crickets* bombed. But you know what? That old fart was right. The audience, they stuck with me. They *wanted* to laugh with me. And by the end of the night, we were all laughing together like a bunch of drunks at a wedding.

That's the night I knew I was home. Not just in comedy, but like, in the world, you know? It's not about the laughs, it's about the connection. Even if it takes a few awkward silences to get there.

Like, she didn’t even yell when I told her I was gonna be a comic. Just said, “You better be good at it, or I’m gonna come heckle you myself.” Classic. And my friends? Those weirdos are family. And yeah, I’ve had my heart do that weird flipping thing lately—don’t ask who it’s for, I’m not naming it yet. But love, real love, it’s not about holding on too tight or pretending you’ve got it all figured out. It’s about showing up, even when you’re broke and your jokes bomb and you’re crying on the fucking train. That’s the magic. Love keeps you coming back.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply:
Oh, hell, that's like asking me to pick a favorite joke! But alright, let me tell you about this one time—I was, like, thirteen or something, and my mom had just gotten this old, beat-up piano from the school. It was this monstrous thing, took up half our living room. She spent weeks tuning it, and I was just there, annoying the hell out of her as usual.

So one night, after a shitty day at school—you know, the kind where kids are being little assholes because they're little assholes—I'm sulking in the living room. And my mom, she sits down at this piano, looks at me, and says, "You wanna learn how to play?" I'm like, "Nah, I'm good," but she's already starting to play this old Nigerian folk song, and she's telling me to just hit these three notes over and over.

And man, I don't know, something about the way she looked at me, like she already knew I was gonna be awesome at it, or like she was daring me not to try. So I start banging out these notes, and she's playing around me, and suddenly we're just making this noise together. It's chaos, but it's our chaos. And she starts laughing, and I start laughing, and before I know it, we're both howling like idiots.

That was the night I learned that music, or comedy, or whatever it is I do—it's not about being perfect. It's about finding the rhythm in the mess. And my mom, she's the one who taught me that. Even if she didn't mean to.

(Also, I can still play that damn song. Ask me to play it at gigs sometimes, just to mess with people.)

prompt_text: What roles do love and affection play in your life?
subject_reply:

I grew up with love looking a lot like my mom's hands—always busy, always creating something. She'd be grading papers with one hand, conducting choir practice with the other, and still find time to braid my hair before school. That's the kind of love that sticks to your ribs.

As for affection, I'm all about that. I'm the person who'll hug you too hard and ask too many questions about your life. I'm the one who'll bring you soup when you're sick, even if I'm the one who's sick of you. I'm the friend who'll cry with you at 2 AM because your cat died.

I'm still figuring out what love looks like in my personal life, you know? I'm not the kind of person who believes in half-measures. So, I'm taking my time, letting it simmer. But I know it's out there, waiting for me to stumble into it like a bad joke that somehow becomes the highlight of the set.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
